name: Yogi Berra
description: The gloriously tangled wisdom of baseball's greatest philosopher, one Yogi-ism at a time.
author: Quips Editorial

quotes.content: It ain't over 'til it's over.
quotes.source: The Yogi Book: I Really Didn't Say Everything I Said (1998)
quotes.notes: Berra confirms in The Yogi Book he said it as Mets manager in 1973, though the exact contemporary press record from that season is thin.

quotes.content: It's deja vu all over again.
quotes.source: The Yogi Book: I Really Didn't Say Everything I Said (1998)
quotes.notes: Berra dated it to watching Mantle and Maris hit back-to-back home runs; he confirmed the line as his own in The Yogi Book.

quotes.content: When you come to a fork in the road, take it.
quotes.source: The Yogi Book: I Really Didn't Say Everything I Said (1998)
quotes.notes: Berra says it was directions to his Montclair home, reachable either way; a similar phrasing appears in print before him, but he claims this instance as his.

quotes.content: Nobody goes there anymore. It's too crowded.
quotes.source: The Yogi Book: I Really Didn't Say Everything I Said (1998)
quotes.notes: Berra's remark about Ruggeri's restaurant in St. Louis while talking with Stan Musial and Joe Garagiola, as recounted in The Yogi Book.

quotes.content: You can observe a lot by watching.
quotes.source: The Yogi Book: I Really Didn't Say Everything I Said (1998)
quotes.notes: Berra confirmed this line in The Yogi Book and later used it as the title of a 2008 memoir.

quotes.content: The future ain't what it used to be.
quotes.source: The Yogi Book: I Really Didn't Say Everything I Said (1998)
quotes.notes: Berra claimed it in The Yogi Book; the underlying sentiment traces to Paul Valery (1937), but he embraced the line as his own.

quotes.content: It gets late early out there.
quotes.source: The Yogi Book: I Really Didn't Say Everything I Said (1998)
quotes.notes: Berra's description of the autumn shadows creeping across left field at Yankee Stadium, documented in The Yogi Book.

quotes.content: We made too many wrong mistakes.
quotes.source: The Yogi Book: I Really Didn't Say Everything I Said (1998)
quotes.notes: Berra's explanation for a World Series loss, confirmed as his line in The Yogi Book.

quotes.content: I want to thank you for making this day necessary.
quotes.source: Yogi Berra Night, Sportsman's Park, St. Louis (1947)
quotes.notes: Berra's opening line at the 1947 hometown ceremony honoring him, one of his earliest and best-documented Yogi-isms.

quotes.content: Why buy good luggage? You only use it when you travel.
quotes.source: The Yogi Book: I Really Didn't Say Everything I Said (1998)
quotes.notes: Berra's reply to teammates ribbing his worn bags, confirmed in The Yogi Book.

quotes.content: If the people don't want to come out to the ballpark, nobody's going to stop them.
quotes.source: The Yogi Book: I Really Didn't Say Everything I Said (1998)
quotes.notes: Berra's remark on ballpark attendance; a similar line was attributed to impresario Sol Hurok, but Berra claims this phrasing in The Yogi Book.

quotes.content: We were overwhelming underdogs.
quotes.source: The Yogi Book: I Really Didn't Say Everything I Said (1998)
quotes.notes: Berra's line about the Amazin' 1969 Mets, later recounted to pitcher Nolan Ryan and documented in The Yogi Book.

quotes.content: Pair up in threes.
quotes.source: The Yogi Book: I Really Didn't Say Everything I Said (1998)
quotes.notes: Berra's spring-training instruction to his players, confirmed in The Yogi Book.

quotes.content: If the world were perfect, it wouldn't be.
quotes.source: The Yogi Book: I Really Didn't Say Everything I Said (1998)
quotes.notes: A Yogi-ism he confirmed in The Yogi Book and revisited in his 2002 book When You Come to a Fork in the Road, Take It!

quotes.content: I usually take a two hour nap from one to four.
quotes.source: The Yogi Book: I Really Didn't Say Everything I Said (1998)
quotes.notes: A Yogi-ism listed among his confirmed sayings by the Yogi Berra Museum and in The Yogi Book.

quotes.content: Never answer an anonymous letter.
quotes.source: The Yogi Book: I Really Didn't Say Everything I Said (1998)
quotes.notes: One of the Yogi-isms Berra confirmed as his own in The Yogi Book.

quotes.content: 90% of the game is half mental.
quotes.source: The Yogi Book: I Really Didn't Say Everything I Said (1998)
quotes.notes: This exact wording appears in The Yogi Book; it is often paraphrased as 'Baseball is ninety percent mental and the other half is physical.'

quotes.content: You've got to be very careful if you don't know where you are going, because you might not get there.
quotes.source: The Yogi Book: I Really Didn't Say Everything I Said (1998)
quotes.notes: A Yogi-ism Berra confirmed in The Yogi Book.

quotes.content: If you don't know where you are going, you'll end up someplace else.
quotes.source: The Yogi Book: I Really Didn't Say Everything I Said (1998)
quotes.notes: A Yogi-ism Berra confirmed as his own in The Yogi Book.

quotes.content: I really didn't say everything I said.
quotes.source: Newsday interview (Feb. 1986); The Yogi Book (1998)
quotes.notes: Documented in newspapers in February 1986 when Berra joined the Astros, and later adopted as the subtitle of The Yogi Book.

quotes.content: Little League baseball is a very good thing because it keeps the parents off the streets.
quotes.source: The Yogi Book: I Really Didn't Say Everything I Said (1998)
quotes.notes: A Yogi-ism Berra confirmed in The Yogi Book.

quotes.content: Always go to other people's funerals, otherwise they won't come to yours.
quotes.source: The Yogi Book: I Really Didn't Say Everything I Said (1998)
quotes.notes: A Yogi-ism Berra confirmed as his own in The Yogi Book.

quotes.content: It ain't over till the fat lady sings.
quotes.source: Popularly attributed; Berra denied it in 1998
quotes.notes: Attribution axis failed: Berra himself said he never said it, and the line traces to sportscaster Ralph Carpenter in 1976.

quotes.content: You better cut the pizza in four pieces because I'm not hungry enough to eat six.
quotes.source: Popularly attributed
quotes.notes: Attribution and wording axes failed: the joke was in print (credited to Ken Thompson) by 1965, and The Yogi Book's own wording differs from this popular version.

quotes.content: How can you think and hit at the same time?
quotes.source: Disputed; The Yogi Book (1998) vs. Yogi (1961)
quotes.notes: Attribution axis failed: Berra denied saying it in his 1961 autobiography, then claimed it in The Yogi Book, leaving his own record contradictory.

quotes.content: A nickel ain't worth a dime anymore.
quotes.source: Popularly attributed
quotes.notes: Source axis failed: widely repeated by aggregators with no documented interview or firm confirmation of when Berra said it.

quotes.content: We're lost, but we're making good time.
quotes.source: Popularly attributed
quotes.notes: Source axis failed: no documented origin ties this line to a specific Berra utterance.

quotes.content: No matter where you go, there you are.
quotes.source: Popularly attributed
quotes.notes: Attribution axis failed: a common saying of uncertain origin routinely pinned on Berra without documentation.

quotes.content: It ain't the heat, it's the humility.
quotes.source: Popularly attributed
quotes.notes: Attribution axis failed: an older vaudeville-style pun on 'humidity' attached to Berra without a documented source.
